unformatted_files: Code style issues found in 83 files. Run Prettier with --write to fix.

metric_analyses.measures: Static code analysis violations detected by ESLint in JavaScript/TypeScript source code, including potential bugs, style inconsistencies, and anti-patterns
metric_analyses.computation_method: ESLint tool + JSON parser - executes 'npx eslint {src_dirs} --format json' and counts violation entries in parsed JSON output

influencing_factors.factor: New code written or existing code modified
ESLint rules enforced or strictness increased
Developer fixing violations or refactoring code
Dependency updates introducing new peer warnings
Team code review and quality practices

metric_analyses.data_freshness: Per CI/CD run or commit; changes immediately when code is modified

metric_analyses.measures: Number of source code files that do not conform to Prettier code formatting standards
metric_analyses.computation_method: Prettier tool + check mode parser - executes 'npx prettier --check {src_dirs}' and counts files failing formatting validation

influencing_factors.factor: New code written without automatic formatting applied
Prettier configuration changes (width, quotes, semi-colons, etc.)
Automatic formatting executed (pre-commit hooks or formatting pass)
Merge conflicts with unresolved formatting inconsistencies
IDE/editor Prettier plugin auto-formatting on save
Code generation tools respecting Prettier formatting

metric_analyses.data_freshness: Per CI/CD run or commit; changes immediately when code is modified

dependencies.explanation: ESLint configs commonly integrate Prettier via eslint-plugin-prettier or eslint-config-prettier. When present (likely given dashboard/eslint.config.js exists), every file failing Prettier formatting also generates ESLint violations for the same formatting issues. Fixing prettier_violations via 'npx prettier --write' would automatically eliminate the overlapping ESLint formatting rules in one pass, reducing eslint_violations without any manual code changes.

Both metrics are outputs of the same underlying condition: inconsistent code hygiene discipline across contributors or automated formatting not being enforced in CI/pre-commit hooks. Files accumulating ESLint violations (anti-patterns, unused imports, etc.) tend to also lack formatting enforcement, meaning high eslint_violations is a strong predictor of elevated prettier_violations. They share common root causes rather than a direct causal link in this direction.

After running 'npx prettier --write' to zero out prettier_violations, any remaining eslint_violations are purely logical/semantic (unused variables, type errors, anti-patterns) rather than formatting noise. This separation makes the residual eslint_violations more actionable and higher-signal — each remaining violation represents a real code quality risk rather than a style artifact.

risk_clusters.risk: Absent or unenforced pre-commit hooks / CI formatting gate: The project spans three independent sub-packages (dashboard/, server/, desktop-electron/) each capable of drifting independently. Without a unified Husky + lint-staged or CI check enforcing both tools on every commit, violations accumulate per-package at different rates. The scripts/patches/SonaTrajectoryService.js plain-JS file is especially at risk of being excluded from both ESLint and Prettier scope configs, creating a permanent blind spot.

TSX component surface area (21 components in dashboard/src/components/): React component files are the highest-churn area of the codebase. ESLint violations here (missing dependency arrays in useEffect, prop-types issues, unused imports from refactors) carry runtime risk — not just style debt. Violations in components like SessionView.tsx, ProjectDashboard.tsx, or EventStream.tsx could mask subtle bugs that ESLint's react-hooks plugin would otherwise surface.

Multi-runtime TypeScript boundary risk: The codebase mixes browser (dashboard/), Node.js/Electron main process (desktop-electron/src/main.ts, preload.ts), and Express server (server/src/) runtimes. If ESLint environment rules are misconfigured per package, violations may be suppressed in the wrong context (e.g., browser globals leaking into server lint scope). Prettier violations compounding across all three packages inflate the total count non-linearly if each package has separate ignore or config files.

doc_understanding.application_purpose: OctoAlly is a local-first web dashboard for orchestrating AI coding sessions powered by Claude Code and RuFlo. It lets developers launch, monitor, and manage single-agent and multi-agent (hive-mind) coding sessions, interactive terminals, and git workflows from a single real-time UI.
target_users: Software developers using Claude Code for AI-assisted coding
Power users running multi-agent RuFlo hive-mind sessions
Teams wanting a unified UI to manage multiple concurrent AI coding workflows
key_workflows: Launch and monitor single-agent Claude Code sessions
Run multi-agent hive-mind sessions via RuFlo with Byzantine consensus
Manage interactive terminal sessions with tmux persistence (pop-out and adopt back)
Browse and test web apps in the built-in browser alongside coding sessions
Review, stage, and commit AI-generated code changes via built-in git integration
Explore and edit project files via the built-in file explorer
Dictate instructions via local Whisper STT or cloud STT APIs
Install and manage OctoAlly as a system service (systemd/launchd)
Switch release channels (stable/beta/canary) and apply updates via CLI
Define and launch custom per-project agent personas from .claude/agents/*.md
known_limitations: Only Linux (Ubuntu/Debian) and macOS are supported — no Windows support
Requires Node.js 20+ and a pre-initialized Claude Code installation; Claude Code must be run interactively at least once to accept terms before OctoAlly can use it non-interactively

The Commons Clause in the license prohibits selling products or services whose value derives substantially from OctoAlly itself, and any product incorporating OctoAlly source code must be distributed free of charge
Multi-agent hive-mind capability depends on the external RuFlo project, introducing an additional dependency and a potential single point of failure
Authentication is optional (OCTOALLY_TOKEN left empty for local use), meaning the dashboard is unauthenticated by default — a risk if exposed beyond localhost
No documented role-based access control or multi-user support
Desktop app is Electron-only; there is no documented mobile or remote-access path beyond the web dashboard
Session state is stored in a local SQLite database — no documented backup, replication, or export mechanism for session history
Cloud STT API keys are stored encrypted on disk (AES-256-GCM) but there is no documented key-rotation or revocation workflow
No documented Windows support path even for the web dashboard only (server-side)
improvement_opportunities: Default unauthenticated access: The docs state OCTOALLY_TOKEN can be left empty for local use, with no enforcement or warning. Add a setup wizard that prompts users to set a token on first launch, or auto-generate one, to prevent accidental exposure when the server is reachable on a LAN.

No bulk session management: There is no documented way to stop, restart, or archive multiple sessions at once. Add a multi-select action in the Active Sessions Grid (e.g., 'Stop all idle sessions') to reduce manual overhead.

No session export or history search: Session output is streamed and displayed, but there is no documented endpoint or UI to export session logs or search historical output. Add a log export (JSON/plaintext) button per session and a full-text search over stored session output in SQLite.

Missing Windows support: Only Linux and macOS are listed as supported. Since the stack (Node.js, Fastify, tmux) has Windows equivalents (e.g., ConPTY), documenting or implementing a Windows path (even without tmux persistence) would broaden the user base.

No health/status endpoint documented: There is no documented REST endpoint for checking server health (e.g., GET /healthz). Adding one would enable monitoring integrations and service managers to detect crashes without scraping logs.

Error messages from failed agent sessions are not described: The docs do not mention what users see when a Claude Code session fails to start (e.g., missing API key, Claude Code not initialized). Surface structured error messages in the dashboard with remediation hints (e.g., 'Claude Code not found — run: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code').

No documented API reference: The server exposes REST and WebSocket endpoints (inferred from the architecture), but there is no API reference in the docs. Publish an OpenAPI/Swagger spec so power users and integrators can script against OctoAlly without reading source code.

RuFlo initialization is automatic but opaque: The docs say OctoAlly 'automatically initializes' RuFlo when a project is enabled, but do not describe what files are created or how to customize/reset them. Add a 'View RuFlo config' panel in the project settings and document the generated file structure.

No multi-user or team support: There is a single shared dashboard with no user accounts, permissions, or audit log. For team use, add at minimum a read-only viewer role and an audit log of session launches and commits made through the UI.

AES-256-GCM key management is undocumented: API keys for cloud STT are encrypted at rest, but there is no documentation on where the encryption key is stored, how to rotate it, or what happens if it is lost. Add a 'Manage secrets' section in the docs and UI covering key location, rotation, and recovery.

No documented update rollback: The CLI has an 'update' command and release channels, but there is no documented rollback procedure if a canary or beta update breaks the server. Add a 'rollback to previous version' CLI subcommand or document the manual rollback steps.

tmux/dtach as hard runtime dependencies: Session persistence requires tmux and dtach to be installed on the host, but the install script only checks for Node.js and Claude Code. Add explicit pre-flight checks for tmux and dtach with install instructions when they are missing.

proposals.title: Fix Prettier formatting in server service files
proposals.description: Run `npx prettier --write` on the five largest server-side service files. These files are the most likely sources of Prettier violations given their size (session-manager.ts alone is 2177 lines). Prettier will normalise quote styles, trailing commas, bracket spacing, and line widths without altering runtime behaviour.
acceptance_criteria: npx prettier --check on all five files exits 0 with no formatting warnings
No changes to exported symbols, function signatures, or runtime logic
All existing tests (if any) continue to pass

proposals.title: Fix Prettier formatting in server route and config files
proposals.description: Run `npx prettier --write` on the remaining larger server-side route and config files. projects.ts (643 lines) and git.ts (371 lines) are particularly likely to carry formatting drift from multi-author edits.
acceptance_criteria: npx prettier --check on all five files exits 0
No runtime logic, exported types, or function signatures are altered
Server starts cleanly after the change

proposals.title: Fix Prettier formatting in large dashboard component files
proposals.description: Run `npx prettier --write` on the five largest dashboard React components. FileExplorer.tsx (1540 lines), ProjectDashboard.tsx (1754 lines), ProjectView.tsx (1562 lines), ModelSettingsModal.tsx (1143 lines), and GitPanel.tsx (782 lines) together represent a high proportion of the total dashboard codebase and are prime candidates for accumulated formatting drift.
acceptance_criteria: npx prettier --check on all five files exits 0
No JSX structure, prop types, or component logic is altered
Dashboard renders without errors in the browser after the change

proposals.title: Fix Prettier formatting in remaining dashboard component files
proposals.description: Run `npx prettier --write` on the next tier of dashboard components: Terminal.tsx (793 lines), SessionLauncher.tsx (722 lines), NewTaskForm.tsx (735 lines), DiffComponents.tsx (613 lines), and AgentGuide.tsx (577 lines). These mid-sized files collectively cover a wide surface area of the UI.
acceptance_criteria: npx prettier --check on all five files exits 0
No component logic, event handlers, or JSX structure is changed
Dashboard renders and all interactive elements function correctly

proposals.title: Fix Prettier formatting in desktop-electron speech pipeline files
proposals.description: Run `npx prettier --write` on the desktop Electron speech subsystem files. speech/index.ts (988 lines), speech/commands.ts (452 lines), speech/whisper.ts (308 lines), speech/audio.ts (246 lines), and main.ts (412 lines) make up the bulk of the Electron layer and are likely to have inconsistent formatting given the complex async logic they contain.
acceptance_criteria: npx prettier --check on all five files exits 0
No changes to class method signatures, async control flow, or IPC handler registrations
Electron app launches and speech features work correctly after the change

proposals.title: Fix Prettier formatting in remaining dashboard and scripts files
proposals.description: Run `npx prettier --write` on the App.tsx entry point, ActiveTerminals.tsx, SessionView.tsx, scripts/patches/SonaTrajectoryService.js, and dashboard/src/App.tsx to mop up any remaining formatting violations not covered by P-001 through P-005. SonaTrajectoryService.js (646 lines) is a standalone patch script that often escapes linting discipline.
acceptance_criteria: npx prettier --check on all five files exits 0
npx prettier --check . (project-wide) exits 0 after applying P-001 through P-006 in sequence
prettier_violations metric reaches 0

proposals.title: Pre-flight checks for tmux and dtach with actionable install hints
proposals.target_outcome: When tmux or dtach are missing at server startup, OctoAlly fails fast with a human-readable error message and platform-specific install instructions, rather than silently degrading or producing cryptic spawn errors later when a session is launched.
proposals.description: server/src/config.ts already contains a `binaryAvailable` helper and the `wantDtach`/`wantTmux` derived flags, but their absence is never surfaced to the user at startup — the server continues to start and the failure only appears as a runtime error inside `pty-worker.ts` when an actual session spawn is attempted. The fix is to call `binaryAvailable` for both `tmux` and `dtach` inside the `start()` function in `server/src/index.ts`, and if either required binary is absent, emit a structured warning (or fatal error when the backend is configured to require them) that includes the exact `apt`/`brew` install command for the detected platform. A corresponding banner in the dashboard (`dashboard/src/components/ProjectView.tsx` or `SessionLauncher.tsx`) can surface the warning from a server health endpoint so web-only users also see it before they attempt to launch a session.
acceptance_criteria: Starting the server without tmux installed logs a clearly labelled error with 'sudo apt install tmux' (Linux) or 'brew install tmux' (macOS) before the server enters its listening state
Starting the server without dtach installed logs the equivalent install hint when dtach persistence is enabled
The SessionLauncher component shows an inline warning banner (not a crash) when the server reports a missing dependency
When both binaries are present the startup path is unchanged and no new log output is emitted

proposals.title: Bulk session stop action in the Active Sessions Grid
proposals.target_outcome: Users can select multiple running sessions and stop them all with a single 'Stop selected' action, eliminating the need to open and kill each session individually when cleaning up idle or stale agent runs.
proposals.description: The Active Sessions Grid rendered inside `ProjectView.tsx` currently only supports per-card actions (open, hide, kill). There is no multi-select mode. The proposal adds a checkbox-style selection layer to the session cards in `ProjectView.tsx` (toggled by a 'Select' toolbar button to avoid cluttering the default view), a 'Stop selected' and 'Hide selected' action bar that appears when one or more sessions are checked, and a lightweight server-side bulk endpoint in `server/src/routes/sessions.ts` that accepts an array of session IDs and calls `killSession` for each. The existing `killSession` export from `session-manager.ts` is reused directly — no new kill logic is needed. `dashboard/src/lib/api.ts` receives one new `bulkKillSessions(ids: string[])` helper that POSTs to the new endpoint.
acceptance_criteria: A 'Select' toggle button appears in the sessions grid toolbar; clicking it shows a checkbox on each session card
Selecting at least one session reveals a 'Stop selected (N)' action button
Clicking 'Stop selected' sends a single request and kills all chosen sessions; the grid updates without a full page reload
A 'Hide selected' action moves chosen sessions to the hidden list, mirroring the existing single-session hide behaviour
Selecting zero sessions (or deselecting all) hides the action bar and returns to normal view
Sessions that are already stopped or in an error state are excluded from the stop call without causing a request failure

proposals.title: First-launch authentication token setup prompt
proposals.target_outcome: New users are guided to set or auto-generate an authentication token on first launch instead of being left with an unauthenticated server, reducing the risk of accidental LAN exposure without requiring manual environment variable configuration.
proposals.description: The docs acknowledge OCTOALLY_TOKEN can be left empty for local use, but the server emits no warning and the dashboard shows no indication that it is running unauthenticated. The proposal adds a first-run detection flag (a sentinel written to the SQLite DB via `server/src/db/index.ts` after the first successful startup) and a `/api/auth/setup` endpoint in `server/src/index.ts` that is accessible without a token only when the flag indicates setup has not yet completed. On the dashboard side, `dashboard/src/App.tsx` checks a new `GET /api/auth/status` endpoint on mount; if the response indicates `{ configured: false }`, it renders a non-dismissable `SetupTokenModal` component before the main dashboard is shown. The modal offers two options: 'Generate a token for me' (server-side `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')`, persisted to the environment file or a local config file) and 'I'll set OCTOALLY_TOKEN manually' (shows the env var name and docs link). Once a token is configured the flag is set, the modal never appears again, and the standard Bearer token check in the request pipeline is enforced. No new npm dependencies are required — `crypto` is a Node built-in.
acceptance_criteria: On first launch with OCTOALLY_TOKEN unset, the dashboard displays a blocking setup modal before any project or session content is shown
The modal's 'Generate token' action calls the setup endpoint, receives the generated token, and displays it with a copy button and a reminder to store it
After token generation the modal is dismissed and does not appear on subsequent launches
The 'Set manually' path shows the exact environment variable name and a link to the relevant docs section, then allows the user to confirm and reload
When OCTOALLY_TOKEN is already set at startup the sentinel flag is written immediately and the setup modal is never shown
The /api/auth/setup and /api/auth/status endpoints are not reachable once setup is complete (return 404 or 403)
